content.problemStatement:
Chef has a sequence AAA of 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N integers containing all natural numbers from 111 to NNN **exactly twice**.

Let a sequence BBB containing KKK elements be a subsequence of AAA. We define a **strictly increasing** index sequence I(B)=[I1,I2,…,IK]I(B) = [I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_K]I(B)=[I1​,I2​,…,IK​] such that, BBB can be written as [AI1,AI2,…,AIK][A_{I_1}, A_{I_2}, \ldots, A_{I_K}][AI1​​,AI2​​,…,AIK​​].

A pair of subsequences (P,Q)(P,Q)(P,Q) is said to be good if all of the following conditions satisfy:


Both subsequences have the same length. In other words, ∣P∣=∣Q∣|P| = |Q|∣P∣=∣Q∣, where ∣P∣|P|∣P∣ and ∣Q∣|Q|∣Q∣ denote the lengths of PPP and QQQ respectively;
The subsequences PPP and QQQ are disjoint. In other words, there are **no** common elements between I(P)I(P)I(P) and I(Q)I(Q)I(Q);
The subsequences are equal. In other words, Pj=QjP_j = Q_jPj​=Qj​ for all (1≤j≤∣P∣)(1\le j \le |P|)(1≤j≤∣P∣).
Find the total number of **distinct ordered** pairs of subsequences (P,Q)(P,Q)(P,Q) that are good.

Two ordered pairs (P1,Q1)(P_1, Q_1)(P1​,Q1​) and (P2,Q2)(P_2, Q_2)(P2​,Q2​) are considered different iff I(P1)≠I(P2)I(P_1) \ne I(P_2)I(P1​)=I(P2​) or I(Q1)≠I(Q2)I(Q_1) \ne I(Q_2)I(Q1​)=I(Q2​).

Since the answer can be very large, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

First line contains TTT − the number of test cases. The description of the test cases is as follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN − the value of NNN.
The second line of each test case contains 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N integers A1,A2,…,A2⋅NA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{2 \cdot N}A1​,A2​,…,A2⋅N​​.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer − the total number of **distinct ordered** pairs of subsequences (P,Q)(P,Q)(P,Q) that are good modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 6⋅1056\cdot 10^56⋅105.
